1: Φοίβην τὴν ἀδελφὴν ἡμῶν (Phoebe our sister) — the kinship designation adelphē marks full membership in the covenant community, not a subordinate category. οὖσαν καὶ διάκονον τῆς ἐκκλησίας (who is also a deacon/servant of the church, G1249) — diakonos has no feminine form in Greek; it applies without modification to Phoebe. The word is used for Christ in 15:8, for Paul in 1 Cor 3:5, and for community officers in Phil 1:1 and 1 Tim 3:8. The same term for the same role, regardless of the referent's gender.
2: ἀξίως τῶν ἁγίων (in a manner worthy of the saints) — the genitive of standard: the quality of reception is calibrated to what membership in the holy people deserves. προστάτις πολλῶν ἐγενήθη καὶ ἐμοῦ αὐτοῦ (she has been a patron/helper of many and of me myself, G4368) — prostatis (G4368) is the feminine of prostates, a term from civic life for a patron or sponsor who provides legal support and financial backing for persons lacking status. Paul explicitly names himself as among her beneficiaries, confirming the substantive nature of the role.
3: Πρίσκαν καὶ Ἀκύλαν (Priscilla and Aquila) — Priscilla is named first; across the six NT references to this couple, she is named first in four. Ancient convention generally placed higher-status individuals first; the consistent pattern likely reflects her leadership role. τοὺς συνεργούς μου ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ (my co-workers in Christ Jesus, G4904) — synergos is Paul's term for collaborative ministry partners (cf. Phil 4:3; 1 Thess 3:2); applied equally here to Priscilla and Aquila together.
4: τὸν ἑαυτῶν τράχηλον ὑπέθηκαν (they laid down their own necks, G5137) — an idiom for risking execution; the image is of baring one's throat to a blade. The reflexive heautōn emphasizes the personal cost. πᾶσαι αἱ ἐκκλησίαι τῶν ἐθνῶν (all the churches of the Gentiles) — a broad acknowledgment indicating that Priscilla and Aquila's sacrifice was known and appreciated across the Pauline network.
5: τὴν κατ᾽ οἶκον αὐτῶν ἐκκλησίαν (the church in their house, G1577) — the house-church is the primary organizational unit of early Christianity; there is no dedicated church building in this period. ἀπαρχὴν τῆς Ἀσίας (first fruit of Asia, G536) — the same term Paul uses for the Spirit (8:23) and for the resurrection (1 Cor 15:20); applying it to an individual describes Epaenetus as the initial yield of the gospel harvest in the province, consecrating the whole to God.
6: ἥτις πολλὰ ἐκοπίασεν εἰς ὑμᾶς (who labored much for you, G2872) — kopiaō is Paul's word for intensive labor (used for agricultural and manual work); it appears frequently in his accounts of apostolic ministry (1 Cor 15:10; Gal 4:11; Phil 2:16). The application of this term to Mary affirms the substantive and effortful nature of her contribution to the Roman community.
7:
Ἀνδρόνικον καὶ Ἰουνίαν (Andronicus and Junia) — the second name is almost certainly Iounian (accusative of Iounia, a common Roman female name) rather than Iounian (accusative of the unattested male name Iounias); the female reading is supported by the standard Greek accent and by the absence of the name Junias in any ancient source. ἐπίσημοι ἐν τοῖς ἀποστόλοις (outstanding among the apostles) — the phrase is debated: either (a) notable/distinguished within the group of apostles, or (b) well-known in the eyes of the apostles. Option (a) would make Junia an apostle; option (b) would not. The lexical parallel with other en-constructions favors the inclusive reading.

οἳ καὶ πρὸ ἐμοῦ γέγοναν ἐν Χριστῷ (who also were in Christ before me) — the perfect tense gegonasin indicates a state persisting from before Paul's conversion; they are senior to him in the faith.
8: τὸν ἀγαπητόν μου ἐν κυρίῳ (my beloved in the Lord) — the en kyriō qualifier is consistent throughout the greeting list; it specifies that the love and relationship is grounded in their shared belonging to Christ, not merely social affinity.
9: Οὐρβανὸν τὸν συνεργὸν ἡμῶν ἐν Χριστῷ (Urbanus our co-worker in Christ) — the synergos title again (cf. v.3); its consistent use marks those who have participated directly in the gospel mission alongside Paul.
10: τὸν δόκιμον ἐν Χριστῷ (the approved/tested one in Christ, G1384) — dokimos (the positive of adokimos, the disqualified mind of 1:28) means one who has stood the test, proven genuine. τοὺς ἐκ τῶν Ἀριστοβούλου (those from the household of Aristobulus) — not Aristobulus himself (who may not have been a believer) but members of his household.
11: τὸν συγγενῆ μου (my kinsman/fellow Jew, G4773) — syngenēs can mean blood relative or ethnic kin (fellow Jew); given the usage pattern in this section, ethnic kinship (fellow Jew) is more likely.
12: Τρύφαιναν καὶ Τρυφῶσαν τὰς κοπιώσας ἐν κυρίῳ (Tryphena and Tryphosa, those who work hard in the Lord, G2872) — kopiaousas (present participle) again: both women are characterized by ongoing labor in the community. The names mean "dainty/soft" (Tryphena) and "delicate" (Tryphosa) — possibly sisters, given the similar names. Περσίδα τὴν ἀγαπητήν, ἥτις πολλὰ ἐκοπίασεν ἐν κυρίῳ (beloved Persis, who labored much in the Lord) — the aorist ekopiasein for Persis vs. present participle for Tryphena/Tryphosa may indicate that Persis's intensive work was in the past, while the others' continues.
13: Ῥοῦφον τὸν ἐκλεκτὸν ἐν κυρίῳ (Rufus, the chosen one in the Lord, G1588) — eklektos (elect/chosen) is the standard term for divine election; whether it is a common designation here for any believer or a specific honorific is unclear. τὴν μητέρα αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐμοῦ (his mother and mine) — the biological mother of Rufus who has functioned as a mother to Paul; a profound statement of the re-family that the new covenant creates.
14: The four names in v.14 are followed by τοὺς σὺν αὐτοῖς ἀδελφούς (the brothers/siblings with them) — indicating a house-church or small group attached to these individuals. The pattern repeats in v.15. Multiple overlapping house-churches in Rome (vv.5, 10, 11, 14, 15) reflects the decentralized social organization of the Roman church.
15: The greeting to Philologus, Julia, Nereus, his sister, and Olympas concludes the individual and household greetings. πάντων τῶν ἁγίων τῶν σὺν αὐτοῖς (all the saints with them) — the community Paul is addressing is distributed across multiple households; the letter functions as communication to the whole network.
16: φιλήματι ἁγίῳ (with a holy kiss, G5370) — the greeting practice appears in all four major Pauline letters (1 Cor 16:20; 2 Cor 13:12; 1 Thess 5:26); its designation as "holy" distinguishes it from secular social kissing by marking it as a covenant-community act. αἱ ἐκκλησίαι πᾶσαι τοῦ Χριστοῦ (all the churches of Christ) — the final greeting extends the apostolic network's endorsement to the Roman community.
17: σκοπεῖν τοὺς τὰς διχοστασίας καὶ τὰ σκάνδαλα ποιοῦντας (watch out for those making divisions and stumbling blocks, G1370/G4625) — skopeō (G4648) is attentive watching, not passive observation; dichostasia (divisions) and skandala (stumbling-blocks, traps) are the double threat. παρὰ τὴν διδαχὴν ἣν ὑμεῖς ἐμάθετε (contrary to the teaching you learned) — the aorist emathete treats the received teaching as the stable norm against which deviations are measured.
18: τῇ ἑαυτῶν κοιλίᾳ δουλεύουσιν (they are enslaved to their own belly/appetite, G2836) — koilia (stomach/belly) is used contemptuously for self-indulgent, appetite-driven behavior (cf. Phil 3:19); these teachers serve their own material interests under religious cover. χρηστολογίας καὶ εὐλογίας (smooth talk and flattery, G5543/G2129) — chrēstologia is a rare compound (G5543) meaning plausible, fair-sounding speech; eulogia here in the unusual negative sense of fine-sounding empty praise. The two terms describe the rhetorical technique of the troublemakers.
19: ἐφ᾽ ὑμῖν χαίρω (I rejoice over/because of you) — epi + dative marks the ground of joy: the Roman community's obedience itself. θέλω δὲ ὑμᾶς σοφοὺς εἶναι εἰς τὸ ἀγαθόν (but I want you to be wise for good) — the infinitive construction specifies the direction of wisdom: toward good rather than merely smart. ἀκεραίους δὲ εἰς τὸ κακόν (innocent/unmixed regarding evil, G185) — akeraios means unadulterated, unmixed; the image is of undiluted material not contaminated by evil.
20: ὁ θεὸς τῆς εἰρήνης συντρίψει τὸν Σατανᾶν ὑπὸ τοὺς πόδας ὑμῶν ἐν τάχει (the God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet) — syntribō (G4937) is a strong crushing verb; en tachei (speedily, soon) indicates near-future eschatological expectation. This alludes to Gen 3:15 (the head of the serpent crushed); Paul applies the promise to the community's cosmic struggle with the adversary. The God of peace crushing the adversary is not paradoxical: peace requires the defeat of what opposes it.
21: Τιμόθεος ὁ συνεργός μου (Timothy my co-worker, G4904) — the first of the sender's greetings from Paul's companions; synergos used for Timothy as in v.3 for Priscilla and Aquila. Λούκιος καὶ Ἰάσων καὶ Σωσίπατρος οἱ συγγενεῖς μου (Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater, my kinsmen) — the kinship designation likely means fellow Jews (cf. v.11) rather than blood relatives.
22: ἐγὼ Τέρτιος ὁ γράψας τὴν ἐπιστολὴν (I, Tertius, who wrote the letter) — a rare moment when the professional scribe (amanuensis) identifies himself; Paul would have dictated and Tertius wrote. The phrase ἐν κυρίῳ (in the Lord) characterizes Tertius's own greeting, indicating he was a believer, not merely a hired secretary.
23: Γάϊος ὁ ξένος μου καὶ ὅλης τῆς ἐκκλησίας (Gaius my host and of the whole church, G3581) — xenos here means host (one who receives guests), not stranger; Gaius's house served as Paul's base and the gathering place for the whole church in Corinth. Ἔραστος ὁ οἰκονόμος τῆς πόλεως (Erastus the city treasurer/steward, G3623) — oikonomos tēs poleōs is the financial administrator of the city; an inscription found at Corinth mentions an Erastus who paved a street at his own expense. If the same person, his conversion is socially significant.
24: ἡ χάρις τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ) — the standard Pauline benediction; some manuscripts omit v.24 as textually suspect (it may be a scribal insertion assimilating to the Thessalonian epistles). The doxology that follows (vv.25-27) is the letter's formal close.
25: τῷ δὲ δυναμένῳ ὑμᾶς στηρίξαι (now to him who is able to establish you, G4741) — the doxology opens with the divine capacity to do what Paul has been praying for throughout the letter (cf. 1:11). κατὰ τὸ εὐαγγέλιόν μου καὶ τὸ κήρυγμα Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (according to my gospel and the proclamation of Jesus Christ) — the dual genitive: the gospel as Paul proclaims it, and the proclamation whose content is Jesus Christ. κατὰ ἀποκάλυψιν μυστηρίου χρόνοις αἰωνίοις σεσιγημένου (according to the revelation of mystery silent in ages past, G3466) — mystērion in Paul is not an esoteric secret but a divinely hidden plan now disclosed; sesigēmenou (perfect passive participle = having been kept silent) emphasizes the completed divine concealment now ending.
26: φανερωθέντος δὲ νῦν (but now having been disclosed, G5319) — the aorist passive marks the disclosure as the eschatological event; nyn is the same temporal marker as 3:21 (nyni = but now). διά τε γραφῶν προφητικῶν (also through prophetic writings) — the mystery is disclosed both through the gospel event and through the OT Scriptures; the letter's entire argument about the Law and Prophets bearing witness (3:21) is here summarized. εἰς ὑπακοὴν πίστεως (for the obedience of faith) — the closing bracket of the letter's framing (cf. 1:5); the same phrase that described Paul's mission purpose in the salutation now describes the goal of the mystery's disclosure to all nations.
27: μόνῳ σοφῷ θεῷ (to the only wise God) — the dative of indirect object of the glory-ascription. Monō sophō (only wise) contrasts the transcendent wisdom of God with the pretended wisdom of 1:22 ("claiming to be wise, they became fools"). The letter closes where it began: the gospel as the disclosure of divine wisdom to those who were without excuse before God's wisdom in creation. διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (through Jesus Christ) — the mediatorial role persists even in the doxological address; glory reaches God through and in Christ, not apart from him.
